edges.description: Constitutively active FGFR2 sustains downstream MAPK/ERK signaling.
edges.target: Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in suture osteoblasts

edges.description: ERK/MAPK signaling drives the suture osteogenic program.
edges.source: Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in suture osteoblasts

edges.description: Excess suture osteogenesis precipitates premature suture fusion.
Premature suture fusion manifests clinically as craniosynostosis.

nodes.description: ERK1/2-driven FGFR2 signaling accelerates osteoblast differentiation and matrix mineralization within cranial suture mesenchyme.

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 promotes osteogenic differentiation in mesenchymal cells via ERK1/2 and protein kinase C signaling.
evidence.snippet: Both WT and MT FGFR2 increased early and late osteoblast gene expression and matrix mineralization.

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 promotes osteogenic differentiation in mesenchymal cells via ERK1/2 and protein kinase C signaling.
evidence.snippet: Using dominant-negative ERK and PKCalpha vectors, we demonstrated that WT and MT FGFR2 promoted osteoblast gene expression through ERK1/2 and PKCalpha signaling, respectively.

nodes.description: Variable craniosynostosis affecting one or more sutures.

evidence.reference_title: Jackson-Weiss and Crouzon syndromes are allelic with mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor 2.
evidence.snippet: Jackson-Weiss syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition characterized by craniosynostosis, foot anomalies and great phenotypic variability.

nodes.description: Mutations in FGFR2 cause constitutive receptor activation, leading to premature cranial suture fusion and abnormal skeletal development in the feet. The specific mutations overlap with those causing Crouzon and Pfeiffer syndromes, with phenotype depending on genetic background and other modifying factors.

evidence.reference_title: Jackson-Weiss and Crouzon syndromes are allelic with mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor 2.
evidence.snippet: We now report an FGFR2 mutation in the conserved region of the immunoglobulin IIIc domain in the Jackson-Weiss syndrome family in which the syndrome was originally described.

evidence.reference_title: Jackson-Weiss syndrome: identification of two novel FGFR2 missense mutations shared with Crouzon and Pfeiffer craniosynostotic disorders.
evidence.snippet: Both nucleotide changes predict a serine for cysteine-342 substitution in the second half of the third immunoglobulin-like domain.

nodes.description: Excess osteogenic activity prematurely fuses one or more cranial sutures, producing the variable craniosynostosis that, together with the foot anomalies, defines Jackson-Weiss syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: Jackson-Weiss and Crouzon syndromes are allelic with mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor 2.
evidence.snippet: Jackson-Weiss syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition characterized by craniosynostosis, foot anomalies and great phenotypic variability.

nodes.description: Constitutively active FGFR2 sustains MAPK/ERK signaling in osteoblast-lineage cells of the cranial suture, the conserved effector branch shared across the FGFR craniosynostoses.
nodes.id: Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in suture osteoblasts

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 promotes osteogenic differentiation in mesenchymal cells via ERK1/2 and protein kinase C signaling.
evidence.snippet: WT and MT FGFR2 induced ERK1/2 but not JNK or PI3K/AKT phosphorylation.
